title: Creating Your Next.js App
intro: You're ready to create the Next.js project for your task manager. It's like picking your safety gear before a climb — the choices you make during setup will shape how easy the project is to work with. Let's see how you handle it.

teach.title: create-next-app Does the Heavy Lifting
teach.body: The command `npx create-next-app@latest` scaffolds a full Next.js project with sensible defaults. It asks you a series of questions about TypeScript, ESLint, Tailwind CSS, and the App Router. For our task manager, we want TypeScript (catches bugs early), ESLint (catches mistakes), Tailwind (styles fast), and the App Router (the modern way to build Next.js apps).

teach.title: The IKEA Flatpack vs. Raw Lumber
teach.body: Using create-next-app is like buying an IKEA flatpack — all the pieces are cut, the screws are included, and the instructions are right there. Manual setup is like going to a lumberyard and cutting every piece yourself. You CAN do it, but why would you when the flatpack gets you a functional desk in 30 minutes?

teach.title: Choosing Your Safety Gear Before the Climb
teach.body: Picking TypeScript, ESLint, and Tailwind during setup is like choosing a helmet, harness, and good shoes before rock climbing. You COULD climb without them, but one slip and you're in trouble. These tools catch you when you fall — TypeScript spots type errors, ESLint flags bad patterns, and Tailwind keeps your styles organized. Put the gear on before you start climbing, not halfway up the wall.

teach.title: Why These Defaults Matter
teach.body: TypeScript adds types to JavaScript, which means your editor can warn you about bugs before you even run the code. Tailwind CSS lets you style components without switching between files. The App Router uses the `app/` directory and gives you layouts, loading states, and server components out of the box.

You just learned how to spin up a professional-grade project in under a minute. Freelancers charge clients hundreds of dollars just for project setup — you can now do this in your sleep and start building the product that earns.
Always use create-next-app to start a Next.js project. Pick TypeScript, ESLint, and Tailwind CSS. These aren't optional extras — they're tools that prevent bugs and save time from day one.

start.narrative: You open your terminal to create the Next.js project for your task manager. What command do you run?

choices.text: mkdir task-manager && npm init -y && npm install next react react-dom
Search GitHub for 'next.js task manager template' and clone someone else's project

options-screen.narrative: create-next-app asks you a series of setup questions. It prompts: 'Would you like to use TypeScript?' How do you answer this and the remaining prompts?

choices.text: Yes to TypeScript, Yes to ESLint, Yes to Tailwind, Yes to App Router, No to src/ directory
No to TypeScript because it looks complicated. Yes to everything else.

manual-setup.narrative: You've manually installed the packages, but now you have no config files, no TypeScript setup, no linting, and no project structure. You'll need to create next.config.js, tsconfig.json, and set up ESLint manually. What do you do?

choices.text: Delete this folder and rerun create-next-app with the recommended defaults
Spend the next hour configuring everything by hand

cloned-template.narrative: You cloned a template from 2022. It uses the Pages Router (deprecated pattern), has 47 dependencies you don't understand, and the README says 'Work in progress, do not use in production.' You realize you need a clean slate.

choices.text: Delete the cloned repo and run create-next-app instead
Try to upgrade the template's dependencies and migrate to the App Router yourself

first-run.narrative: Install finishes. You cd into the project and need to confirm everything works before you start coding. What's your first move inside the new folder?

choices.text: Run 'npm run dev' and open http://localhost:3000 to confirm the welcome page renders
Open package.json and read every dependency before running anything
Start deleting files like the default favicon and example styles before running the app once

explore-folders.narrative: The dev server is up. Now you want to find where to add your task manager UI. The scaffold gave you app/, public/, and a few config files. Where do you put your first edit?

choices.text: Edit app/page.tsx — that's the homepage rendered by the App Router
Edit app/layout.tsx and replace the body content with a homepage
Create a pages/ directory at the root and add an index.tsx file there

next-steps.narrative: Your edit shows up in the browser on save. Before you start building features, what should you set up so you don't lose work?

choices.text: Confirm git is initialized, write a quick README, and make a clean first commit of the scaffold
Skip git for now and start building — you'll commit once there's something interesting to save
Install ten libraries you might need later (drag-and-drop, charts, animations) before writing any feature

excellent-end.narrative: You picked the official CLI, accepted the recommended defaults, verified the dev server boots, edited the right file, and locked in a clean git baseline. Your task manager has a professional foundation and you can start building features with confidence.
excellent-end.feedback: Textbook setup. create-next-app + TypeScript + Tailwind + App Router + a verified dev run + a first commit is exactly the runway every Next.js project should have. Future you will thank present you every time you reach for a config that's already correct.

mediocre-end.narrative: Your project boots and you've started editing app/page.tsx, but without git you have no save points. The first time you delete the wrong file or break a layout, you'll be reconstructing it from memory.
mediocre-end.feedback: The scaffold itself is fine, but skipping the first commit means you've already given up your safety net. Make the initial commit before the second feature — every minute of unversioned code is a minute of risk.

weak-end.narrative: You spent an hour installing libraries you don't need yet, your package.json is bloated, and you still haven't written a single feature. When TypeScript complains about a peer dep mismatch, you don't know which install caused it.
weak-end.feedback: Premature dependencies are technical debt before you've even shipped a feature. Add libraries when a real requirement appears, not 'just in case.' Start lean, prove the feature works, then install what you actually need.
